items.q: What is the purpose of database normalisation?
items.correct: To reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies.
items.misconception: To make the database run faster.
items.random_wrong: To make the database look neater.
items.why: Students think normalisation is about performance. In fact, heavy normalisation often slows queries. Its purpose is DATA INTEGRITY - preventing inconsistencies by storing each fact once.

items.q: What does an INNER JOIN return?
items.correct: Only rows with matching values in both tables.
items.misconception: All rows from both tables combined.
items.random_wrong: Only the inner columns of each table.
items.why: Students confuse INNER (intersection) with FULL OUTER (union). INNER drops unmatched rows from BOTH sides; LEFT keeps all from left; RIGHT keeps all from right; FULL OUTER keeps everything.

items.q: What is hexadecimal A in denary?
items.why: Students forget hex uses A-F to represent 10-15 because we need 16 distinct symbols. A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15.

items.q: Which attack tries every password combination?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Brute force. “Phishing” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Network traffic filter. “Antivirus signature” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many factors are checked in two-factor authentication?
items.why: The real figure is 2 factors. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
